perturbation.original_text: 1.3 C onfidential Information shall be identified either by marking it, in the case of written materials, or, in the case of information that is disclosed orally or written materials that are not marked, by notifying the other Party of the confiden tial nature of the information. Such notification shall be done orally, by e -mail or written correspondence, or via other appropriate means of communication.
perturbation.changed_text: 1.3 Sensitive Information shall be identified either by marking it, in the case of written materials, or, in the case of information that is disclosed orally or written materials that are not marked, by notifying the other Party of the confiden tial nature of the information. Such notification shall be done orally, by e -mail or written correspondence, or via other appropriate means of communication.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'Confidential Information' to 'Sensitive Information' in this section introduces a contradiction. The agreement defines 'Confidential Information' specifically in 1.1. Using 'Sensitive Information' here without defining it creates confusion about whether it's the same as, a subset of, or different from 'Confidential Information'.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 1.1 For the purposes of this Agreement, Confidential I nformation means any data or information that is pr oprietary to or possessed by a P arty and not generally known to the public or that has not yet been revealed, whether in tangible or intangible form, whenever and however disclosed, including, but not limited to: (i) any scientific or technical information, invention, design, process, procedure, formula, improvement, technology or method; (ii) any concepts, ideas, samples, reports, data, know -how, works -in-progress, designs, drawings, photographs, development tools, speci fications, software programs, source code, object code, flow charts, and databases; (iii) any marketing strategies, plans, financial information, or projections, operations, sales estimates, business plans and performance results relating to the Party’s past, p resent or future business activities, or those of its affiliates, subsidiaries and affiliated companies; (iv) trade secrets; plans for products or services, and customer or supplier lists; (v) any other information that should reasonably be recognised as Confide ntial Information by the Parties.
perturbation.justification: The change from 'Confidential Information' to 'Sensitive Information' in section 1.3 creates a contradiction because section 1.1 specifically defines 'Confidential Information'. By using 'Sensitive Information' without defining it, the modified text introduces ambiguity and uncertainty, potentially conflicting with the defined meaning of 'Confidential Information' in section 1.1. This aligns with the Misaligned Terminology definition.

perturbation.original_text: 3.6 All Confidential Information disclosed under this Agreement shall b e and remain the property of the Disclosing Party and nothing contained in this Agreement shall be construed as granting or conferring any rights to such Confidential Information on the other Party. Principally, nothing in this Agreement shall be deemed to grant to the Receiving Party a licence expressly or by implication under any patent, copyright or other intellectual property right. The Receiving Party hereby acknowledges and confirms that all existing and future intellectual property rights related to the Confidential Information are exclusive titles of the Disclosing Party. For the sake of clarity based in reciprocity and good faith of the Parties, the Receiving Party will not apply for or obtain any intellectual property protection in respect of the C onfidential Information received. Likewise any modifications and improvements thereof by the Receiving Party shall be the sole property of the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.changed_text: 3.6 All Confidential Information disclosed under this Agreement shall b e and remain the property of the Disclosing Party and nothing contained in this Agreement shall be construed as granting or conferring any rights to such Confidential Information on the other Party. Principally, nothing in this Agreement shall be deemed to grant to the Receiving Party a licence expressly or by implication under any patent, copyright or other intellectual property right. The Receiving Party hereby acknowledges and confirms that all existing and future intellectual property rights related to the Confidential Information are exclusive titles of the Disclosing Party. For the sake of clarity based in reciprocity and good faith of the Parties, the Receiving Party will not apply for or obtain any intellectual property protection in respect of the Proprietary Information received. Likewise any modifications and improvements thereof by the Receiving Party shall be the sole property of the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.explanation: The change from 'Confidential Information' to 'Proprietary Information' in the last sentence of this section is another instance of introducing undefined terminology. While both terms aim to protect sensitive data, 'Proprietary Information' is not defined within the agreement, leading to potential disputes over its scope and whether modifications and improvements made by the Receiving Party are actually subject to the same restrictions as 'Confidential Information'.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 3.6 All Confidential Information disclosed under this Agreement shall b e and remain the property of the Disclosing Party and nothing contained in this Agreement shall be construed as granting or conferring any rights to such Confidential Information on the other Party. Principally, nothing in this Agreement shall be deemed to grant to the Receiving Party a licence expressly or by implication under any patent, copyright or other intellectual property right. The Receiving Party hereby acknowledges and confirms that all existing and future intellectual property rights related to the Confidential Information are exclusive titles of the Disclosing Party. For the sake of clarity based in reciprocity and good faith of the Parties, the Receiving Party will not apply for or obtain any intellectual property protection in respect of the Proprietary Information received. Likewise any modifications and improvements thereof by the Receiving Party shall be the sole property of the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.justification: The change from 'Confidential Information' to 'Proprietary Information' introduces an undefined term. Section 3.6 defines obligations related to 'Confidential Information,' but the changed text applies those obligations to 'Proprietary Information' without defining what constitutes 'Proprietary Information'. This creates a contradiction because the receiving party's obligations regarding modifications and improvements are now tied to an undefined term, making it unclear whether modifications of Confidential Information are governed by the original definition or some other standard. The document does not define Proprietary Information, which misaligns terminology.
